title: Deep Blue Beats Kasparov
caption: One of Deep Blue's two towers — the IBM machine that beat world champion Garry Kasparov in 1997 — on display at the Computer History Museum.

overview.text: In May 1997, IBM's chess computer Deep Blue defeated reigning world champion Garry Kasparov 3.5–2.5 in a six-game match — the first time a computer had beaten a world chess champion under standard tournament conditions. Deep Blue evaluated up to 200 million positions per second using custom VLSI chips and a combination of alpha-beta search, opening book knowledge, and an endgame tablebase. Kasparov famously accused IBM of cheating, a claim the match transcripts do not support.

deep.text: Deep Blue's strength came from brute-force alpha-beta pruning across a massive search tree, accelerated by 480 custom chess chips. Its evaluation function encoded decades of grandmaster knowledge as hand-tuned heuristics for material balance, king safety, pawn structure and piece mobility. The system is not a learning system in the modern sense; its power was engineering depth and domain-specific hardware.

challenge.prompt: Put these AI milestones in chronological order.

feedback.correct: Perfect — you've got the arc from the first artificial neuron right through to the chess championship.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite. The order is: McCulloch–Pitts neuron (1943) → Dartmouth conference (1956) → Perceptron (1958) → Deep Blue (1997).

sources.title: Deep Blue
sources.author: M. Campbell, A. J. Hoane Jr. & F. Hsu
